cases.title: Pain That Moved
cases.presentation: A 22-year-old woman describes vague periumbilical pain that started two days ago and has now settled into a sharp pain in her right lower quadrant, worse with movement.
cases.question: What is the most likely diagnosis based on this pain pattern?
options: B: Appendicitis — the classic migration from periumbilical to right lower quadrant pain.
D: Simple gastroenteritis, no further concern.
cases.explanation: The migration from vague periumbilical pain to focal right lower quadrant pain over roughly two days is the classic appendicitis pattern, distinguishing it from conditions that are focal from onset.
cases.clinicalPearl: Pain that starts in the middle and moves to the lower right is appendicitis until proven otherwise.

cases.title: Missed Dialysis, New Weakness
cases.presentation: A 55-year-old man on hemodialysis missed his last two scheduled sessions due to transportation issues. He now reports generalized weakness and tingling in his hands and feet.
cases.question: What should be your top concern and immediate action?
options: A: Simple fatigue — no specific workup needed.
B: Hyperkalemia — place on a cardiac monitor immediately given the risk of dangerous dysrhythmias, and transport urgently.
C: A stroke, unrelated to his dialysis status.
D: Anxiety related to missing appointments.
cases.explanation: Missed dialysis strongly raises the risk of hyperkalemia, which presents with weakness and paresthesias and can produce dangerous cardiac dysrhythmias — his irregular, slow pulse is an additional red flag. Cardiac monitoring and urgent transport are indicated.
cases.clinicalPearl: Missed dialysis plus weakness is a cardiac emergency in waiting — get him on the monitor.

cases.title: Not Just Heartburn
cases.presentation: A 62-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes reports 'bad indigestion' with epigastric discomfort and nausea for the past hour. He denies classic chest pain.
cases.question: Given his risk factors, what should be done despite the absence of classic chest pain?
options: A: Treat as simple indigestion with antacids only.
B: Obtain a 12-lead ECG to rule out inferior MI presenting atypically as epigastric discomfort.
C: Assume this is unrelated to his heart since there is no chest pain.
cases.explanation: Inferior MI can present with epigastric discomfort and nausea instead of classic chest pain, especially in patients with diabetes (which can blunt typical pain perception) and hypertension. A 12-lead ECG is warranted before assuming a purely GI cause.
cases.clinicalPearl: In a patient with cardiac risk factors, 'indigestion' gets a 12-lead before it gets antacids.
